questions.question: In the default provider order registered by `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)`, which source has the **highest** precedence on a key collision?
options: Command-line arguments
`appsettings.json`
Environment variables
`appsettings.{Environment}.json`
questions.explanation: The default order is appsettings.json ‚Üí appsettings.{Environment}.json ‚Üí user secrets (Dev) ‚Üí environment variables ‚Üí command-line args, with last-wins per key. Command-line args are registered last, so they have the highest default precedence.

questions.question: An environment variable needs to set the config key `ConnectionStrings:Default`. How must it be named?
options: `ConnectionStrings__Default` (double underscore maps to colon)
`ConnectionStrings:Default` (colons are allowed in env vars everywhere)
`ConnectionStrings--Default` (double dash maps to colon)
`ConnectionStrings.Default` (dot maps to colon)
questions.explanation: Because colons aren't valid in environment variable names on some shells, the env var provider translates a double underscore `__` to the colon separator. The `--`‚Üí`:` mapping is specific to Azure Key Vault secret names, not env vars.

questions.question: Which options interface is registered as a **singleton**, computes its value **once**, and never reloads?
options: `IOptions<T>`
`IOptionsSnapshot<T>`
`IOptionsMonitor<T>`
`IConfigureOptions<T>`
questions.explanation: `IOptions<T>` is a singleton whose value is computed once and cached forever ‚Äî it never reloads and has no named-options support. `IOptionsSnapshot<T>` is scoped and `IOptionsMonitor<T>` is a singleton that does track live changes.

questions.question: The .NET Secret Manager (`dotnet user-secrets`) encrypts secrets at rest so they are safe to store anywhere on disk.
questions.explanation: User Secrets are stored as plaintext JSON in the user profile, outside the project tree. Its purpose is keeping secrets out of source control ‚Äî not at-rest encryption.

questions.question: Calling `configuration.GetSection("DoesNotExist")` on a section that is not present in any provider returns `null`.
questions.explanation: `GetSection` never returns null ‚Äî a missing section yields an empty section. To know whether it was actually present you must call `section.Exists()`.

questions.question: A class registered as a **singleton** takes a constructor dependency on `IOptionsSnapshot<MyOptions>`. What happens?
options: It throws an exception about consuming a scoped service from a singleton (captive dependency)
It works fine and the snapshot reloads on every request as usual
It silently injects the default (unnamed) options and never reloads
It throws only if `ValidateOnStart()` was called on the options
questions.explanation: `IOptionsSnapshot<T>` is registered scoped; capturing a scoped service in a singleton is a captive dependency, and the DI scope validator throws `InvalidOperationException`. The correct fix for singletons/hosted services is `IOptionsMonitor<T>`.

questions.question: A value is supplied via an **environment variable** and consumed through `IOptionsMonitor<T>`. The env var's value is changed in the OS while the app is running. What does `CurrentValue` reflect?
options: The original startup value ‚Äî env vars are read once at startup and don't provide change tokens
The new value immediately ‚Äî `IOptionsMonitor<T>` always reflects live changes from any provider
The new value on the next request only, like a snapshot
An exception, because monitors require `reloadOnChange` on every provider
questions.explanation: Reload depends on the provider emitting change tokens. Only file providers added with `reloadOnChange` watch their source; environment variables and command-line args are read once at startup and never hot-reload, so `CurrentValue` stays at the original value.

questions.question: You registered `Configure<SmtpOptions>("Backup", ...)`. Which is the correct way to retrieve the "Backup" configuration?
options: Inject `IOptionsSnapshot<SmtpOptions>` (or `IOptionsMonitor<SmtpOptions>`) and call `.Get("Backup")`
Inject `IOptions<SmtpOptions>` and call `.Get("Backup")`
Inject `IOptions<SmtpOptions>`; `.Value` returns the "Backup" options because it was registered last
Inject `IConfiguration` and call `.GetNamedOptions("Backup")`
questions.explanation: Named options are retrieved via `IOptionsSnapshot<T>.Get(name)` or `IOptionsMonitor<T>.Get(name)`. `IOptions<T>` has no concept of names ‚Äî it only ever returns the default (unnamed) options.

questions.question: A developer typos a key as `"Smpt:Host"` in `appsettings.json` instead of `"Smtp:Host"`. With default binder settings, what is the observable behavior?
options: The `Host` property silently keeps its default value; no error is raised
Binding throws an exception about an unknown configuration key
The app fails to start because the key doesn't match the section
`GetSection("Smtp")` returns null, surfacing the typo immediately
questions.explanation: By default unmatched config keys are silently ignored, so the property keeps its default and the typo causes a 'my setting isn't taking effect' bug. Setting `BinderOptions.ErrorOnUnknownConfiguration = true` makes it fail loudly.

questions.question: Options are registered with `.ValidateDataAnnotations()` but **without** `.ValidateOnStart()`. When does validation actually run?
options: Lazily, the first time the options are resolved (`.Value`/`.Get()`), which may be deep in a request
Eagerly during `builder.Build()`, before any request is handled
On every request, regardless of whether the options are accessed
Never ‚Äî `ValidateDataAnnotations()` only registers attributes; you must call `Validate(...)` to run them
questions.explanation: Validation is lazy by default ‚Äî it runs the first time the options instance is resolved, which can be long after startup. `.ValidateOnStart()` moves it to application startup so bad config fails fast instead of throwing later on first use.

questions.question: In a unit test, `Options.Create(new SmtpOptions { Host = "x" })` produces an `IOptions<SmtpOptions>` without needing any `IConfiguration` or DI machinery.
questions.explanation: `Options.Create(...)` wraps a plain POCO in an `IOptions<T>`, which is exactly why typed options are more testable than reaching into `IConfiguration` ‚Äî you inject a known object instead of mocking the whole config tree.

questions.question: What config key does `configuration.GetConnectionString("Default")` actually read?
options: `ConnectionStrings:Default`
`Default:ConnectionString`
`ConnectionString:Default`
`Connections:Default`
questions.explanation: `GetConnectionString("Name")` is a shortcut that reads the `ConnectionStrings:Name` key ‚Äî here `ConnectionStrings:Default`.

questions.question: Which statements correctly describe how a single options instance is built? (Select all that apply.)
options: All `Configure`/`Bind` delegates run before any `PostConfigure` delegate
`PostConfigure<T>` runs after all `Configure` calls but before validation
Validation runs last, so it sees the final post-configured value
Validation runs before `PostConfigure`, so post-config can fix invalid values
`PostConfigure` runs before `Configure` to seed defaults that `Configure` then overrides
questions.explanation: The pipeline is: all Configure/Bind delegates ‚Üí all PostConfigure delegates ‚Üí validation last. That ordering means validation evaluates the final, post-configured value; the two reversed-order options are wrong.

questions.question: Given this registration, what is `o.Port` after the options are built if `appsettings.json` has `Smtp:UseSsl = true` and no `Smtp:Port` key?
options: 465 ‚Äî `Port` binds to its default 0, then `PostConfigure` runs after binding and applies 465
25 ‚Äî `PostConfigure` runs before binding, so `UseSsl` is still false when the default is computed
0 ‚Äî `PostConfigure` cannot mutate options that were already bound from configuration
A validation exception, because `Port` is outside the valid range
questions.explanation: Binding runs first and leaves `Port` at its default 0; `PostConfigure` runs afterward, sees `Port == 0` and `UseSsl == true`, and sets 465. PostConfigure always runs after all Configure/Bind delegates, so `UseSsl` is already true.

questions.question: A long-lived singleton subscribes with `IOptionsMonitor<T>.OnChange(...)` in its constructor but never stores or disposes the returned value. What is the senior-level concern?
options: A resource/callback leak ‚Äî the returned `IDisposable` registration is never released, so the subscription lingers
Nothing ‚Äî `OnChange` registrations are garbage-collected automatically when the callback returns
The monitor stops emitting changes after the first callback because the token wasn't renewed
It throws at startup because `OnChange` may only be called from a hosted service
questions.explanation: `OnChange` returns an `IDisposable` representing the subscription; failing to dispose it leaks the registration. The reviewer explicitly notes you should keep the `IDisposable` and dispose it (e.g. in `Dispose`) to avoid leaks.

questions.question: Regarding the Azure Key Vault configuration provider and Managed Identity, which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Key Vault is not one of the default providers; you add it explicitly with `AddAzureKeyVault`
A Key Vault secret named `Smtp--Host` surfaces as the config key `Smtp:Host`
Using `DefaultAzureCredential` (Managed Identity) means no secret is needed to authenticate and read the secrets
Where you add Key Vault in the provider chain has no effect on precedence since vault always wins
Key Vault secret names use `__` (double underscore) to express hierarchy, just like environment variables
questions.explanation: Key Vault must be added explicitly and its chain position determines precedence (so the 'always wins' claim is wrong). Secret names map `--`‚Üí`:` (not `__`, which is the env-var convention). Managed Identity via `DefaultAzureCredential` solves the chicken-and-egg problem with no stored secret.

questions.question: `appsettings.json` defines `Feature:Limit=10` and `Feature:Name=A`. A command-line arg passes `--Feature:Limit=99` but nothing for `Feature:Name`. What are the effective values?
options: `Limit=99`, `Name=A` ‚Äî precedence is last-wins **per key**, not whole-file replacement
`Limit=99`, `Name` unset ‚Äî the CLI provider replaces the entire `Feature` section
`Limit=10`, `Name=A` ‚Äî JSON files always win over command-line args
A binding error, because the CLI provided a partial section
questions.explanation: Precedence is evaluated key-by-key (last-wins per key), so the CLI overrides only `Feature:Limit` while JSON still supplies `Feature:Name`. It is not whole-section replacement.

questions.question: `appsettings.json` defines an array `Servers` with three elements (indices 0‚Äì2). You set the environment variable `Servers__0=override`. What is the result?
options: Only index 0 is overridden; indices 1 and 2 keep their JSON values ‚Äî you can't 'clear' the array via env vars
The entire `Servers` array is replaced by a single-element array `["override"]`
The env var is ignored because arrays can only be set in JSON
A binding exception, because the array length now mismatches the JSON definition
questions.explanation: Arrays are keyed by integer index (`Servers:0`, `Servers:1`, ‚Ä¶), so `Servers__0` overrides only index 0. Env vars can override or extend by index but cannot clear or shrink a JSON-defined array.
